notes.note: Cicero's senatorial interventions against Antony in autumn 44 BC, the beginnings of the Philippics; sententia is the formal opinion delivered when a senator is called on to speak.
Antony publicly accused Cicero of having masterminded the assassination of the dictator Caesar (the Ides of March 44 BC), aiming to turn Caesar's veterans against him.
Caesar's discharged soldiers, settled on land and fiercely loyal to his memory; their resentment was a weapon Antony could turn against the assassins and their supporters.
L. Calpurnius Piso Caesoninus (cos. 58), Caesar's father-in-law, who on 1 August 44 BC was the first senator to speak openly against Antony, with no one supporting him.
Cicero delivered his First Philippic against Antony on 2 September 44 BC, roughly a month after Piso's lone attack.
P. Servilius Vatia Isauricus (cos. 48 BC), an eminent consular who followed Cicero in attacking Antony in the Senate.
A standard term of abuse Cicero hurls at Antony throughout the Philippics, branding him a brawler and cutthroat rather than a statesman.
The Latin dates this a. d. xiii K. Octobr. (the thirteenth day before the Kalends of October), the day Antony delivered the speech replying to Cicero's First Philippic.
The country house formerly belonging to Q. Caecilius Metellus Pius Scipio (the Pompeian commander), where Antony was said to have rehearsed his speech amid drink and debauchery.

notes.anchor: not to be speaking but vomiting
notes.note: An allusion to Antony's notorious public vomiting in the assembly while drunk, a charge Cicero made memorable in the Second Philippic.
Consulars (former consuls) carried the greatest weight in the Senate; Cicero means himself, Piso, and Servilius, the only ones who dared speak against Antony and so could not attend safely.

Magistracies were dated by the names of the year's consuls; an ambitious adherent of Antony hoped to secure the consulship for his son in the year already earmarked (by Caesar's arrangements) for Brutus and Cassius.
L. Aurelius Cotta (cos. 65), Caesar's uncle, who in despondency stayed away from the Senate after the Ides of March.
L. Iulius Caesar (cos. 64), an upright senator and (paradoxically) Antony's own uncle, kept from the Senate by ill health.
Servius Sulpicius Rufus (cos. 51), the celebrated jurist; he later died as one of the senatorial envoys to Antony in early 43 BC.
The designati, here Hirtius and Pansa, who were to enter the consulship on 1 January 43 BC; consuls-elect already enjoyed special precedence in senatorial debate.
